title: The Sack Of Baltimore
content:
آ آ آ آ The summer sun is falling soft on Carberyï؟½s hundred isles,
آ آ آ آ The summer sun is gleaming still through Gabrielï؟½s rough defiles;
آ آ آ آ Old Innisherkinï؟½s crumbled fane looks like a moulting bird,
آ آ آ آ And in a calm and sleepy swell the ocean tide is heard:
آ آ آ آ The hookers lie upon the beach; the children cease their play;
آ آ آ آ The gossips leave the little inn; the households kneel to pray;
آ آ آ آ And full of love, and peace, and rest, its daily labor oï؟½er,
آ آ آ آ Upon that cosy creek there lay the town of Baltimore.

آ آ آ آ A deeper rest, a starry trance, has come with midnight there;
آ آ آ آ No sound, except that throbbing wave, in earth, or sea, or air!
آ آ آ آ The massive capes and ruinï؟½d towers seem conscious of the calm;
آ آ آ آ The fibrous sod and stunted trees are breathing heavy balm.
آ آ آ آ So still the night, these two long barques round Dunashad that glide
آ آ آ آ Must trust their oars, methinks not few, against the ebbing tide.
آ آ آ آ Oh, some sweet mission of true love must urge them to the shore!
آ آ آ آ They bring some lover to his bride who sighs in Baltimore.

آ آ آ آ All, all asleep within each roof along that rocky street,
آ آ آ آ And these must be the loverï؟½s friends, with gently gliding feetï؟½
آ آ آ آ A stifled gasp, a dreamy noise! ï؟½The roof is in a flame!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ From out their beds and to their doors rush maid and sire and dame,
آ آ آ آ And meet upon the threshold stone the gleaming sabreï؟½s fall,
آ آ آ آ And oï؟½er each black and bearded face the white or crimson shawl.
آ آ آ آ The yell of ï؟½Allah!ï؟½ breaks above the prayer, and shriek, and roar:
آ آ آ آ O blessed God! the Algerine is lord of Baltimore!

آ آ آ آ Then flung the youth his naked hand against the shearing sword;
آ آ آ آ Then sprung the mother on the brand with which her son was gorï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Then sunk the grandsire on the floor, his grand-babes clutching wild;
آ آ آ آ Then fled the maiden moaning faint, and nestled with the child:
آ آ آ آ But see! yon pirate strangled lies, and crushï؟½d with splashing heel,
آ آ آ آ While oï؟½er him in an Irish hand there sweeps his Syrian steel:
آ آ آ آ Though virtue sink, and courage fail, and misers yield their store,
آ آ آ آ There ï؟½s one hearth well avenged in the sack of Baltimore.

آ آ آ آ Midsummer morn in woodland nigh the birds begin to sing,
آ آ آ آ They see not now the milking maids,ï؟½deserted is the spring;
آ آ آ آ Midsummer day this gallant rides from distant Bandonï؟½s town,
آ آ آ آ These hookers crossï؟½d from stormy Skull, that skiff from Affadown;
آ آ آ آ They only found the smoking walls with neighborsï؟½ blood besprent,
آ آ آ آ And on the strewed and trampled beach awhile they wildly went,
آ آ آ آ Then dashï؟½d to sea, and passï؟½d Cape Clear, and saw, five leagues before,
آ آ آ آ The pirate-galley vanishing that ravaged Baltimore.

آ آ آ آ Oh, some must tug the galleyï؟½s oar, and some must tend the steed;
آ آ آ آ This boy will bear a Scheikï؟½s chibouk, and that a Beyï؟½s jerreed.
آ آ آ آ Oh, some are for the arsenals by beauteous Dardanelles;
آ آ آ آ And some are in the caravan to Meccaï؟½s sandy dells.
آ آ آ آ The maid that Bandon gallant sought is chosen for the Dey:
آ آ آ آ She ï؟½s safeï؟½sheï؟½s deadï؟½she stabbï؟½d him in the midst of his Serai!
آ آ آ آ And when to die a death of fire that noble maid they bore,
آ آ آ آ She only smiled, Oï؟½Driscollï؟½s child; she thought of Baltimore.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½T is two long years since sunk the town beneath that bloody band,
آ آ آ آ And all around its trampled hearths a larger concourse stand,
آ آ آ آ Where high upon a gallows-tree a yelling wretch is seen:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½T is Hackett of Dungarvanï؟½he who steerï؟½d the Algerine!
آ آ آ آ He fell amid a sullen shout with scarce a passing prayer,
آ آ آ آ For he had slain the kith and kin of many a hundred there.
آ آ آ آ Some mutterï؟½d of MacMurchadh, who brought the Norman oï؟½er;
آ آ آ آ Some cursï؟½d him with Iscariot, that day in Baltimore.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Thomas Osborne Davis represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Thomas Osborne Davis's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
